author: @PharmaAI-LearningCenter
text: Hello, I mage GO with your method (H2So4 + H3PO4) and I got yellor color compound. On UV spetroscopy I got Peaks at 230 and 300 wavelengths. But in XRD I didn't got any peak means no single peak. Can you suggest me what is happening. Why I am not getting peak in XRD?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
UV-vis spectra of aqueous GO dispersions give two kinds of characteristic features, the first is a shoulder at ∼ 310nm, corresponding to an n-π* plasmon peak. Another characteristic feature appears at 230 nm, and corresponds to a π-π* plasmon peak. The intensity and position of this feature can vary between samples. To identify why the spectra of  GO samples are different, AFM measurements can be obtained to determine the number of layers each GO sample possessed. AFM observations have shown that the thicknesses of chemically derived single-, double-, and triple-layer graphene sheets are approximately 0.57, 1.25 and 1.83 nm. The interlayer spacing is calculated to be in the range of ∼ 0.6-0.7nm. The distributions derived from AFM measurements show that the GO composed of a single layer and, double layers and, and triple layers have an average thickness of about 0.8, 1.3 and 1.8nm, respectively. The interlayer spacing is calculated to be 0.5 nm. Obviously, this value is much larger than that of graphite, which is normally 0.335 nm. Therefore, the interlayer spacing of graphene that has undergone less oxidation than triple layer GO will be 0.335-0.5nm. Considering that the radius of a carbon atom is less than 0.15nm, the increase of thickness caused by the presence of epoxy and hydroxyl groups on bothsurfaces of the carbon atoms is approximately equal to the thickness of single-layer GO. GO containing 10 layers has an average thickness of about 5.9 nm.

XRD limitations tend to be
Homogeneous and single phase material is best for identification of an unknownMust have access to a standard reference file of inorganic compounds (d-spacings, hkls)Requires tenths of a gram of material which must be ground into a powder
For mixed materials, detection limit is ~ 2% of sampleFor unit cell determinations, indexing of patterns for non-isometric crystal systems is complicated
Peak overlay may occur and worsens for high angle 'reflections'
sample prep can be very important
Obtain a few tenths of a gram (or more) of the material, as pure as possible
Grind the sample to a fine powder, typically in a fluid to minimize inducing extra strain (surface energy) that can offset peak
positions, and to randomize orientation. Powder less than ~10 μm(or 200-mesh) in size is preferredPlace into a sample holder or onto the sample surface:
Sprinkle on double sticky tape
Typically the substrate is amorphous to avoid interference care must be taken to create a flat upper surface and to achieve a random
distribution of lattice orientations unless creating an oriented smear.
For analysis of clays and materials which require a single orientation, specialized techniques for preparation of clay samples are given by USGS. - link https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2001/of01-041/htmldocs/methods.htm


For unit cell determinations, a small amount of a standard with known peak positions (that do not interfere with the sample) can be added
and used to correct peak positions.

author: @Potus4547
text:
What about using 2 or more piezoelectric "pistons" to grind the graphene?
The frequency will be ... "insane" :)
Basically we make holes in sphere (like a empty ball) and attach piezoelectric pistons - perpendicular on the sphere.

Inside we put the desired material - and maybe some liquids and we synchronize the whole thing to achieve the "maximum" pressure right in the centre of the sphere - by combining the waves. :)

And voila... a nuke :)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: this method is a modified hummer's and there is a huge variety both in terms of reactants and reactant conditions. That is the amount you use of everything, the temperature you do it at and the amount of time you leave it. i have read some that take only hours. They need constant mixing and the yield isn't that high but still a few hours is tempting. Really, what you need to do is experiment and find the most suitable for you - all of the methods produce GO - it's just a question of how much.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: but it's not the best process - better to filter, then centrifuge, then dry. Howver if that isn't an option, pouring it into excess water and leaving it to precipitate out will work. after that - follow eric - it is a good video that he has.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The GO made by this, and any other method, is just full of contaminants. Mostly sulphates and free metal ions the addition of HCl turns these into soluble salts that can be removed by washing in water. The washing process is really quite tedious - but is necessary to remove the contaminants. you can vacuum filter it if you have the equipment - and this is best - but i try to do experiments that anyone can do at home so i do it with the minimum of equipment - hence my washing process

author: @ragnarokcrusader3
text: I have left the suspension stirring for two days now. The suspension﻿ still feels kinda liquid-like rather than pasty. Tommorow, I will terminate the reaction with peroxide and then perform washing+centrifudge and see how it goes. We would be subjecting the graphene oxide to some test like FTIR and XRD to have a better look at their structure. My main intention in producing the graphene oxide is to use them as a filler in polymer

author: @catjewsus
text: It would be nice if you could link up large amount of cnt into buckypaper so easily~

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: What level is your chemistry - so I know where to pitch my replies to you - The washing with HCl is to get rid of metal ions, centrifuge until it is done! - this is obvious - there will be a lump of stuff at the bottom - if there isn't you need to centrifuge some more. Wash it three times after HCl. It is tedious I am afraid but this is why everybody is looking for an easier method to be able to translate it into an industrial process - good luck and let me know how it goes.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hmm, the chemistry behind this reaction is rich, complex and poorly understood - there is a LOT of debate in the literature about what is going on and three competing models - It is easier to think of it as a process as well as a reaction. In terms of the process the peroxide acts to terminate this. So, in that sense - there is nothing left behind. Have a look at part three which gives some explanation of intercalation as a degree of this process is SO4 intercalation.
